title: Central nervous system

infobox.value: *systema nervosum centrale pars centralis systematis nervosi[[1]](#cite_note-1)*

(**CNS**) is the part of the [nervous system](/wiki/Nervous_system "Nervous system") consisting primarily of the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain"), [spinal cord](/wiki/Spinal_cord "Spinal cord") and [retina](/wiki/Retina "Retina"). The CNS is so named because the brain integrates the received information and coordinates and influences the activity of all parts of the bodies of [bilaterally symmetric and triploblastic animals](/wiki/Bilateria "Bilateria")—that is, all multicellular animals except [sponges](/wiki/Sponge "Sponge") and [diploblasts](/wiki/Coelenterata "Coelenterata"). It is a structure composed of [nervous tissue](/wiki/Nervous_tissue "Nervous tissue") positioned along the [rostral (nose end) to caudal (tail end)](/wiki/Anatomical_terms_of_location#Rostral,_cranial,_and_caudal "Anatomical terms of location") axis of the body and may have an enlarged section at the rostral end which is a brain. Only [arthropods](/wiki/Arthropods "Arthropods"), [cephalopods](/wiki/Cephalopods "Cephalopods") and [vertebrates](/wiki/Vertebrates "Vertebrates") have a true brain, though precursor structures exist in [onychophorans](/wiki/Onychophora "Onychophora"), [gastropods](/wiki/Gastropods "Gastropods") and [lancelets](/wiki/Lancelets "Lancelets").

The **central nervous system** (**CNS**) is the part of the [nervous system](/wiki/Nervous_system "Nervous system") consisting primarily of the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain"), [spinal cord](/wiki/Spinal_cord "Spinal cord") and [retina](/wiki/Retina "Retina"). The CNS is so named because the brain integrates the received information and coordinates and influences the activity of all parts of the bodies of [bilaterally symmetric and triploblastic animals](/wiki/Bilateria "Bilateria")—that is, all multicellular animals except [sponges](/wiki/Sponge "Sponge") and [diploblasts](/wiki/Coelenterata "Coelenterata"). It is a structure composed of [nervous tissue](/wiki/Nervous_tissue "Nervous tissue") positioned along the [rostral (nose end) to caudal (tail end)](/wiki/Anatomical_terms_of_location#Rostral,_cranial,_and_caudal "Anatomical terms of location") axis of the body and may have an enlarged section at the rostral end which is a brain. Only [arthropods](/wiki/Arthropods "Arthropods"), [cephalopods](/wiki/Cephalopods "Cephalopods") and [vertebrates](/wiki/Vertebrates "Vertebrates") have a true brain, though precursor structures exist in [onychophorans](/wiki/Onychophora "Onychophora"), [gastropods](/wiki/Gastropods "Gastropods") and [lancelets](/wiki/Lancelets "Lancelets").

The rest of this article exclusively discusses the vertebrate central nervous system, which is radically distinct from all other animals.

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

In [vertebrates](/wiki/Vertebrates "Vertebrates"), the brain and spinal cord are both enclosed in the [meninges](/wiki/Meninges "Meninges").[[2]](#cite_note-Maton-2) The meninges provide [a barrier](/wiki/Blood%E2%80%93brain_barrier "Blood–brain barrier") to chemicals dissolved in the blood, protecting the brain from most [neurotoxins](/wiki/Neurotoxins "Neurotoxins") commonly found in food. Within the meninges the brain and spinal cord are bathed in [cerebral spinal fluid](/wiki/Cerebral_spinal_fluid "Cerebral spinal fluid") which replaces the [body fluid](/wiki/Extracellular_fluid "Extracellular fluid") found outside the cells of all [bilateral animals](/wiki/Bilaterian "Bilaterian").

In vertebrates, the CNS is contained within the [dorsal body cavity](/wiki/Dorsal_body_cavity "Dorsal body cavity"), while the brain is housed in the [cranial cavity](/wiki/Cranial_cavity "Cranial cavity") within the [skull](/wiki/Skull "Skull"). The spinal cord is housed in the [spinal canal](/wiki/Spinal_canal "Spinal canal") within the [vertebrae](/wiki/Vertebrae "Vertebrae").[[2]](#cite_note-Maton-2) Within the CNS, the interneuronal space is filled with a large amount of supporting non-nervous cells called neuroglia or [glia](/wiki/Glia "Glia") from the Greek for "glue".[[3]](#cite_note-KettenmannFaissner1996-3)

In vertebrates, the CNS also includes the [retina](/wiki/Retina "Retina")[[4]](#cite_note-Purves_2000-4) and the [optic nerve](/wiki/Optic_nerve "Optic nerve") ([cranial nerve](/wiki/Cranial_nerve "Cranial nerve") II),[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald-6) as well as the [olfactory nerves](/wiki/Olfactory_nerves "Olfactory nerves") and [olfactory epithelium](/wiki/Olfactory_epithelium "Olfactory epithelium").[[7]](#cite_note-Gizurarson_S._2012_566–582-7) As parts of the CNS, they connect directly to brain neurons without intermediate [ganglia](/wiki/Ganglion "Ganglion"). The [olfactory epithelium](/wiki/Olfactory_epithelium "Olfactory epithelium") is the only central nervous tissue outside the meninges in direct contact with the environment, which opens up a pathway for [therapeutic](/wiki/Therapeutic "Therapeutic") agents which cannot otherwise cross the meninges barrier.[[7]](#cite_note-Gizurarson_S._2012_566–582-7)

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Structure")]

Main article: [Neuroanatomy](/wiki/Neuroanatomy "Neuroanatomy")

The CNS consists of two major structures: the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain") and [spinal cord](/wiki/Spinal_cord "Spinal cord"). The brain is encased in the skull, and protected by the cranium.[[8]](#cite_note-Moore's-8) The spinal cord is continuous with the brain and lies [caudally](/wiki/Anatomical_terminology "Anatomical terminology") to the brain.[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9) It is protected by the [vertebrae](/wiki/Vertebra "Vertebra").[[8]](#cite_note-Moore's-8) The spinal cord reaches from the base of the skull, and continues through[[8]](#cite_note-Moore's-8) or starting below[[10]](#cite_note-Huijzen-10) the [foramen magnum](/wiki/Foramen_magnum "Foramen magnum"),[[8]](#cite_note-Moore's-8) and terminates roughly level with the first or second [lumbar vertebra](/wiki/Lumbar_vertebra "Lumbar vertebra"),[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Huijzen-10) occupying the upper sections of the [vertebral canal](/wiki/Vertebral_canal "Vertebral canal").[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald-6)

### White and gray matter

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: White and gray matter")]

Main articles: [Gray matter](/wiki/Gray_matter "Gray matter") and [White matter](/wiki/White_matter "White matter")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/1202_White_and_Gray_Matter.jpg/250px-1202_White_and_Gray_Matter.jpg)](/wiki/File:1202_White_and_Gray_Matter.jpg)

Dissection of a human brain with labels showing the clear division between white and gray matter.

Microscopically, there are differences between the neurons and tissue of the CNS and the [peripheral nervous system](/wiki/Peripheral_nervous_system "Peripheral nervous system") (PNS).[[11]](#cite_note-11) The CNS is composed of [white](/wiki/White_matter "White matter") and [gray matter](/wiki/Gray_matter "Gray matter").[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9) This can also be seen macroscopically on brain tissue. The white matter consists of [axons](/wiki/Axon "Axon") and [oligodendrocytes](/wiki/Oligodendrocyte "Oligodendrocyte"), while the gray matter consists of [neurons](/wiki/Neuron "Neuron") and unmyelinated fibers. Both tissues include a number of [glial](/wiki/Glia "Glia") cells (although the white matter contains more), which are often referred to as supporting cells of the CNS. Different forms of glial cells have different functions, some acting almost as scaffolding for [neuroblasts](/wiki/Neuroblast "Neuroblast") to climb during [neurogenesis](/wiki/Neurogenesis "Neurogenesis") such as [bergmann glia](/wiki/Bergmann_glia "Bergmann glia"), while others such as [microglia](/wiki/Microglia "Microglia") are a specialized form of [macrophage](/wiki/Macrophage "Macrophage"), involved in the [immune system](/wiki/Immune_system "Immune system") of the brain as well as the clearance of various [metabolites](/wiki/Metabolite "Metabolite") from the [brain tissue](/wiki/Brain_tissue "Brain tissue").[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald-6) [Astrocytes](/wiki/Astrocyte "Astrocyte") may be involved with both clearance of metabolites as well as transport of fuel and various beneficial substances to neurons from the [capillaries](/wiki/Capillary "Capillary") of the brain. Upon CNS injury astrocytes will proliferate, causing [gliosis](/wiki/Gliosis "Gliosis"), a form of neuronal scar tissue, lacking in functional neurons.[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald-6)

The brain ([cerebrum](/wiki/Cerebrum "Cerebrum") as well as [midbrain](/wiki/Midbrain "Midbrain") and [hindbrain](/wiki/Hindbrain "Hindbrain")) consists of a [cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex"), composed of neuron-bodies constituting gray matter, while internally there is more white matter that form [tracts](/wiki/Nerve_tract "Nerve tract") and [commissures](/wiki/Commissural_fiber "Commissural fiber"). Apart from cortical gray matter there is also [subcortical gray matter](/wiki/Subcortical_gray_matter "Subcortical gray matter") making up a large number of different [nuclei](/wiki/Nucleus_(neuroanatomy) "Nucleus (neuroanatomy)").[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

### Spinal cord

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Spinal cord")]

Main article: [Spinal cord](/wiki/Spinal_cord "Spinal cord")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Sobo_1909_615.png/250px-Sobo_1909_615.png)](/wiki/File:Sobo_1909_615.png)

Diagram of the columns and of the course of the fibers in the spinal cord. Sensory synapses occur in the dorsal spinal cord (above in this image), and motor nerves leave through the ventral (as well as lateral) horns of the spinal cord as seen below in the image.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/1508_Autonomic_Control_of_Pupil_Size.jpg/250px-1508_Autonomic_Control_of_Pupil_Size.jpg)](/wiki/File:1508_Autonomic_Control_of_Pupil_Size.jpg)

Different ways in which the CNS can be activated without engaging the cortex, and making us aware of the actions. The above example shows the process in which the pupil dilates during dim light, activating neurons in the spinal cord. The second example shows the constriction of the pupil as a result of the activation of the Eddinger-Westphal nucleus (a cerebral ganglion).

From and to the spinal cord are projections of the peripheral nervous system in the form of [spinal](/wiki/Spinal_nerve "Spinal nerve") [nerves](/wiki/Nerve "Nerve") (sometimes segmental nerves[[8]](#cite_note-Moore's-8)). The nerves connect the spinal cord to skin, joints, muscles etc. and allow for the transmission of [efferent](/wiki/Efferent_nerve_fiber "Efferent nerve fiber") motor as well as [afferent sensory signals](/wiki/Afferent_nerve_fiber "Afferent nerve fiber") and stimuli.[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9) This allows for voluntary and involuntary motions of muscles, as well as the perception of senses.
All in all 31 spinal nerves project from the brain stem,[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9) some forming plexa as they branch out, such as the [brachial plexa](/wiki/Brachial_plexus "Brachial plexus"), [sacral plexa](/wiki/Sacral_plexus "Sacral plexus") etc.[[8]](#cite_note-Moore's-8) Each spinal nerve will carry both sensory and motor signals, but the nerves synapse at different regions of the spinal cord, either from the periphery to sensory relay neurons that relay the information to the CNS or from the CNS to motor neurons, which relay the information out.[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

The spinal cord relays information up to the brain through spinal tracts through the final common pathway[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9) to the [thalamus](/wiki/Thalamus "Thalamus") and ultimately to the cortex.

#### Cranial nerves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Cranial nerves")]

Apart from the spinal cord, there are also peripheral nerves of the PNS that synapse through intermediaries or [ganglia](/wiki/Ganglion "Ganglion") directly on the CNS. These 12 nerves exist in the head and neck region and are called [cranial nerves](/wiki/Cranial_nerves "Cranial nerves"). Cranial nerves bring information to the CNS to and from the face, as well as to certain muscles (such as the [trapezius muscle](/wiki/Trapezius_muscle "Trapezius muscle"), which is innervated by [accessory nerves](/wiki/Accessory_nerve "Accessory nerve")[[8]](#cite_note-Moore's-8) as well as certain [cervical spinal nerves](/wiki/Cervical_spinal_nerve "Cervical spinal nerve")).[[8]](#cite_note-Moore's-8)

Two pairs of cranial nerves; the [olfactory nerves](/wiki/Olfactory_nerve "Olfactory nerve") and the [optic nerves](/wiki/Optic_nerve "Optic nerve")[[4]](#cite_note-Purves_2000-4) are often considered structures of the CNS. This is because they do not synapse first on peripheral ganglia, but directly on CNS neurons. The olfactory epithelium is significant in that it consists of CNS tissue expressed in direct contact to the environment, allowing for administration of certain pharmaceuticals and drugs.
[[7]](#cite_note-Gizurarson_S._2012_566–582-7)

### Brain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Brain")]

Main article: [Brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain")

At the anterior end of the spinal cord lies the brain.[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9) The brain makes up the largest portion of the CNS. It is often the main structure referred to when speaking of the nervous system in general. The brain is the major functional unit of the CNS. While the spinal cord has certain processing ability such as that of [spinal locomotion](/wiki/Spinal_locomotion "Spinal locomotion") and can process [reflexes](/wiki/Reflex "Reflex"), the brain is the major processing unit of the nervous system.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

#### Brainstem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Brainstem")]

Main article: [Brainstem](/wiki/Brainstem "Brainstem")

The brainstem consists of the [medulla](/wiki/Medulla_oblongata "Medulla oblongata"), the [pons](/wiki/Pons "Pons") and the [midbrain](/wiki/Midbrain "Midbrain"). The medulla can be referred to as an extension of the spinal cord, which both have similar organization and functional properties.[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9) The tracts passing from the spinal cord to the brain pass through here.[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

Regulatory functions of the medulla nuclei include control of [blood pressure](/wiki/Blood_pressure "Blood pressure") and [breathing](/wiki/Breathing "Breathing"). Other nuclei are involved in [balance](/wiki/Balance_(ability) "Balance (ability)"), [taste](/wiki/Taste "Taste"), [hearing](/wiki/Hearing "Hearing"), and control of muscles of the [face](/wiki/Face "Face") and [neck](/wiki/Neck "Neck").[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

The next structure rostral to the medulla is the pons, which lies on the ventral anterior side of the brainstem. Nuclei in the pons include [pontine nuclei](/wiki/Pontine_nuclei "Pontine nuclei") which work with the [cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum") and transmit information between the cerebellum and the [cerebral cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex").[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)
In the dorsal posterior pons lie nuclei that are involved in the functions of breathing, sleep, and taste.[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

The midbrain, or mesencephalon, is situated above and rostral to the pons. It includes nuclei linking distinct parts of the motor system, including the cerebellum, the [basal ganglia](/wiki/Basal_ganglia "Basal ganglia") and both [cerebral hemispheres](/wiki/Cerebral_hemisphere "Cerebral hemisphere"), among others. Additionally, parts of the visual and auditory systems are located in the midbrain, including control of automatic eye movements.[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

The brainstem at large provides entry and exit to the brain for a number of pathways for motor and autonomic control of the face and neck through cranial nerves,[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9) Autonomic control of the organs is mediated by the [tenth cranial nerve](/wiki/Vagus_nerve "Vagus nerve").[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald-6) A large portion of the brainstem is involved in such autonomic control of the body. Such functions may engage the [heart](/wiki/Heart "Heart"), [blood vessels](/wiki/Blood_vessels "Blood vessels"), and [pupils](/wiki/Pupil "Pupil"), among others.[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

The brainstem also holds the [reticular formation](/wiki/Reticular_formation "Reticular formation"), a group of nuclei involved in both [arousal](/wiki/Arousal "Arousal") and [alertness](/wiki/Alertness "Alertness").[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

#### Cerebellum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Cerebellum")]

Main article: [Cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum")

The cerebellum lies behind the pons. The cerebellum is composed of several dividing fissures and lobes. Its function includes the control of posture and the coordination of movements of parts of the body, including the eyes and head, as well as the limbs. Further, it is involved in motion that has been learned and perfected through practice, and it will adapt to new learned movements.[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)
Despite its previous classification as a motor structure, the cerebellum also displays connections to areas of the cerebral cortex involved in language and [cognition](/wiki/Cognition "Cognition"). These connections have been shown by the use of [medical imaging](/wiki/Medical_imaging "Medical imaging") techniques, such as [functional MRI](/wiki/FMRI "FMRI") and [Positron emission tomography](/wiki/Positron_emission_tomography "Positron emission tomography").[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

The body of the cerebellum holds more neurons than any other structure of the brain, including that of the larger [cerebrum](/wiki/Cerebrum "Cerebrum"), but is also more extensively understood than other structures of the brain, as it includes fewer types of different neurons.[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9) It handles and processes sensory stimuli, motor information, as well as balance information from the [vestibular organ](/wiki/Vestibular_organ "Vestibular organ").[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

#### Diencephalon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Diencephalon")]

Main articles: [Diencephalon](/wiki/Diencephalon "Diencephalon"), [Thalamus](/wiki/Thalamus "Thalamus"), and [Hypothalamus](/wiki/Hypothalamus "Hypothalamus")

The two structures of the diencephalon worth noting are the thalamus and the hypothalamus. The thalamus acts as a linkage between incoming pathways from the peripheral nervous system as well as the optical nerve (though it does not receive input from the olfactory nerve) to the cerebral hemispheres. Previously it was considered only a "relay station", but it is engaged in the sorting of information that will reach cerebral hemispheres ([neocortex](/wiki/Neocortex "Neocortex")).[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

Apart from its function of sorting information from the periphery, the thalamus also connects the cerebellum and basal ganglia with the cerebrum. In common with the aforementioned reticular system the thalamus is involved in wakefulness and consciousness, such as though the [SCN](/wiki/Suprachiasmatic_nucleus "Suprachiasmatic nucleus").[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

The hypothalamus engages in functions of a number of primitive emotions or feelings such as [hunger](/wiki/Hunger "Hunger"), [thirst](/wiki/Thirst "Thirst") and [maternal bonding](/wiki/Maternal_bond "Maternal bond"). This is regulated partly through control of secretion of [hormones](/wiki/Hormone "Hormone") from the [pituitary gland](/wiki/Pituitary_gland "Pituitary gland"). Additionally the hypothalamus plays a role in [motivation](/wiki/Motivation "Motivation") and many other behaviors of the individual.[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

#### Cerebrum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Cerebrum")]

Main articles: [Cerebrum](/wiki/Cerebrum "Cerebrum"), [Cerebral cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex"), [Basal ganglia](/wiki/Basal_ganglia "Basal ganglia"), [Amygdala](/wiki/Amygdala "Amygdala"), and [Hippocampus](/wiki/Hippocampus "Hippocampus")

The cerebrum of cerebral hemispheres make up the largest visual portion of the human brain. Various structures combine to form the cerebral hemispheres, among others: the cortex, basal ganglia, amygdala and hippocampus. The hemispheres together control a large portion of the functions of the human brain such as emotion, memory, perception and motor functions. Apart from this the cerebral hemispheres stand for the cognitive capabilities of the brain.[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

Connecting each of the hemispheres is the [corpus callosum](/wiki/Corpus_callosum "Corpus callosum") as well as several additional commissures.[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)
One of the most important parts of the cerebral hemispheres is the [cortex](/wiki/Cortex_(anatomy) "Cortex (anatomy)"), made up of gray matter covering the surface of the brain. Functionally, the [cerebral cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex") is involved in planning and carrying out of everyday tasks.[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

The hippocampus is involved in storage of memories, the amygdala plays a role in perception and communication of emotion, while the basal ganglia play a major role in the coordination of voluntary movement.[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

### Difference from the peripheral nervous system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Difference from the peripheral nervous system")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/1205_Somatic_Autonomic_Enteric_StructuresN.jpg/500px-1205_Somatic_Autonomic_Enteric_StructuresN.jpg)](/wiki/File:1205_Somatic_Autonomic_Enteric_StructuresN.jpg)

A map over the different structures of the nervous systems in the body, showing the CNS, [PNS](/wiki/Peripheral_nervous_system "Peripheral nervous system"), [autonomic nervous system](/wiki/Autonomic_nervous_system "Autonomic nervous system"), and [enteric nervous system](/wiki/Enteric_nervous_system "Enteric nervous system").

The PNS consists of neurons, axons, and [Schwann cells](/wiki/Schwann_cells "Schwann cells"). Oligodendrocytes and Schwann cells have similar functions in the CNS and PNS, respectively. Both act to add [myelin](/wiki/Myelin "Myelin") sheaths to the axons, which acts as a form of insulation allowing for better and faster proliferation of electrical signals along the nerves. Axons in the CNS are often very short, barely a few millimeters, and do not need the same degree of isolation as peripheral nerves. Some peripheral nerves can be over 1 meter in length, such as the nerves to the big toe. To ensure signals move at sufficient speed, myelination is needed.

The way in which the Schwann cells and oligodendrocytes myelinate nerves differ. A Schwann cell usually myelinates a single axon, completely surrounding it. Sometimes, they may myelinate many axons, especially when in areas of short axons.[[8]](#cite_note-Moore's-8) Oligodendrocytes usually myelinate several axons. They do this by sending out thin projections of their [cell membrane](/wiki/Cell_membrane "Cell membrane"), which envelop and enclose the axon.

## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Development")]

[![CNS seen in a median section of a 5-week-old embryo.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Sobo_1909_621.png/330px-Sobo_1909_621.png)](/wiki/File:Sobo_1909_621.png)

[![CNS seen in a median section of a 3-month-old embryo.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Sobo_1909_622.png/330px-Sobo_1909_622.png)](/wiki/File:Sobo_1909_622.png)

Top image: CNS as seen in a median section of a 5-week-old embryo. Bottom image: CNS seen in a median section of a 3-month-old embryo.

Main article: [Neural development](/wiki/Neural_development "Neural development")

During early development of the vertebrate embryo, a longitudinal [groove](/wiki/Neural_groove "Neural groove") on the [neural plate](/wiki/Neural_plate "Neural plate") gradually deepens and the ridges on either side of the groove (the [neural folds](/wiki/Neural_folds "Neural folds")) become elevated, and ultimately meet, transforming the groove into a closed tube called the [neural tube](/wiki/Neural_tube "Neural tube").[[14]](#cite_note-14) The formation of the neural tube is called [neurulation](/wiki/Neurulation "Neurulation"). At this stage, the walls of the neural tube contain proliferating [neural stem cells](/wiki/Neural_stem_cells "Neural stem cells") in a region called the [ventricular zone](/wiki/Ventricular_zone "Ventricular zone"). The neural stem cells, principally [radial glial cells](/wiki/Radial_glial_cell "Radial glial cell"), multiply and generate [neurons](/wiki/Neuron "Neuron") through the process of [neurogenesis](/wiki/Neurogenesis "Neurogenesis"), forming the rudiment of the CNS.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The [neural tube](/wiki/Neural_tube "Neural tube") gives rise to both [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain") and [spinal cord](/wiki/Spinal_cord "Spinal cord"). The anterior (or 'rostral') portion of the neural tube initially differentiates into three brain [vesicles](/wiki/Vesicle_(embryology) "Vesicle (embryology)") (pockets): the [prosencephalon](/wiki/Prosencephalon "Prosencephalon") at the front, the [mesencephalon](/wiki/Mesencephalon "Mesencephalon"), and, between the mesencephalon and the spinal cord, the [rhombencephalon](/wiki/Rhombencephalon "Rhombencephalon"). (By six weeks in the human embryo) the prosencephalon then divides further into the [telencephalon](/wiki/Telencephalon "Telencephalon") and [diencephalon](/wiki/Diencephalon "Diencephalon"); and the rhombencephalon divides into the [metencephalon](/wiki/Metencephalon "Metencephalon") and [myelencephalon](/wiki/Myelencephalon "Myelencephalon"). The spinal cord is derived from the posterior or 'caudal' portion of the neural tube.

As a vertebrate grows, these vesicles differentiate further still. The telencephalon differentiates into, among other things, the [striatum](/wiki/Striatum "Striatum"), the [hippocampus](/wiki/Hippocampus "Hippocampus") and the [neocortex](/wiki/Neocortex "Neocortex"), and its cavity becomes the [first and second ventricles](/wiki/Lateral_ventricles "Lateral ventricles") (lateral ventricles). Diencephalon elaborations include the [subthalamus](/wiki/Subthalamus "Subthalamus"), [hypothalamus](/wiki/Hypothalamus "Hypothalamus"), [thalamus](/wiki/Thalamus "Thalamus") and [epithalamus](/wiki/Epithalamus "Epithalamus"), and its cavity forms the [third ventricle](/wiki/Third_ventricle "Third ventricle"). The [tectum](/wiki/Tectum "Tectum"), [pretectum](/wiki/Pretectum "Pretectum"), [cerebral peduncle](/wiki/Cerebral_peduncle "Cerebral peduncle") and other structures develop out of the mesencephalon, and its cavity grows into the [mesencephalic duct](/wiki/Mesencephalic_duct "Mesencephalic duct") (cerebral aqueduct). The metencephalon becomes, among other things, the [pons](/wiki/Pons "Pons") and the [cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum"), the myelencephalon forms the [medulla oblongata](/wiki/Medulla_oblongata "Medulla oblongata"), and their cavities develop into the [fourth ventricle](/wiki/Fourth_ventricle "Fourth ventricle").[[9]](#cite_note-Kandel-9)

#### Planaria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Planaria")]

[Planarians](/wiki/Planarian "Planarian"), members of the phylum [Platyhelminthes](/wiki/Platyhelminthes "Platyhelminthes") (flatworms), have the simplest, clearly defined delineation of a nervous system into a CNS and a [PNS](/wiki/Peripheral_nervous_system "Peripheral nervous system").[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)
Their primitive brains, consisting of two fused anterior ganglia, and longitudinal nerve cords form the CNS. Like vertebrates, have a distinct CNS and PNS. The nerves projecting laterally from the CNS form their PNS.

A molecular study found that more than 95% of the 116 genes involved in the nervous system of planarians, which includes genes related to the CNS, also exist in humans.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

#### Arthropoda

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Arthropoda")]

In [arthropods](/wiki/Arthropods "Arthropods"), the [ventral nerve cord](/wiki/Ventral_nerve_cord "Ventral nerve cord"), the [subesophageal ganglia](/wiki/Subesophageal_ganglia "Subesophageal ganglia") and the [supraesophageal ganglia](/wiki/Supraesophageal_ganglia "Supraesophageal ganglia") are usually seen as making up the CNS. Arthropoda, unlike vertebrates, have inhibitory [motor neurons](/wiki/Motor_neurons "Motor neurons") due to their small size.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

See also: [Lateral horn of insect brain](/wiki/Lateral_horn_of_insect_brain "Lateral horn of insect brain")

#### Chordata

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Chordata")]

The CNS of [chordates](/wiki/Chordate "Chordate") differs from that of other animals in being placed [dorsally](/wiki/Dorsal_nerve_cord "Dorsal nerve cord") in the body, above the gut and [notochord](/wiki/Notochord "Notochord")/[spine](/wiki/Vertebral_column "Vertebral column").[[20]](#cite_note-Romer-20) The basic pattern of the CNS is highly conserved throughout the different species of [vertebrates](/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate") and during evolution. The major trend that can be observed is towards a progressive telencephalisation: the [telencephalon](/wiki/Telencephalon "Telencephalon") of reptiles is only an appendix to the large [olfactory bulb](/wiki/Olfactory_bulb "Olfactory bulb"), while in mammals it makes up most of the volume of the CNS. In the human brain, the telencephalon covers most of the [diencephalon](/wiki/Diencephalon "Diencephalon") and the entire [mesencephalon](/wiki/Mesencephalon "Mesencephalon"). Indeed, the [allometric](/wiki/Allometry "Allometry") study of brain size among different species shows a striking continuity from rats to whales, and allows us to complete the knowledge about the evolution of the CNS obtained through [cranial endocasts](/wiki/Cranial_endocast "Cranial endocast").

##### Mammals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Mammals")]

[Mammals](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal") – which appear in the fossil record after the first fishes, amphibians, and reptiles – are the only vertebrates to possess the evolutionarily recent, outermost part of the [cerebral cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex") (main part of the telencephalon excluding olfactory bulb) known as the [neocortex](/wiki/Neocortex "Neocortex").[[21]](#cite_note-MarkFBear-21) This part of the brain is, in mammals, involved in higher thinking and further processing of all senses in the [sensory cortices](/wiki/Sensory_cortex "Sensory cortex") (processing for smell was previously only done by its bulb while those for non-smell senses were only done by the [tectum](/wiki/Tectum "Tectum")).[[22]](#cite_note-22) The neocortex of [monotremes](/wiki/Monotremes "Monotremes") (the duck-billed [platypus](/wiki/Platypus "Platypus") and several species of [spiny anteaters](/wiki/Echidna "Echidna")) and of [marsupials](/wiki/Marsupials "Marsupials") (such as [kangaroos](/wiki/Kangaroo "Kangaroo"), [koalas](/wiki/Koala "Koala"), [opossums](/wiki/Opossum "Opossum"), [wombats](/wiki/Wombat "Wombat"), and [Tasmanian devils](/wiki/Tasmanian_devil "Tasmanian devil")) lack the convolutions – [gyri](/wiki/Gyri "Gyri") and [sulci](/wiki/Sulcus_(neuroanatomy) "Sulcus (neuroanatomy)") – found in the neocortex of most [placental mammals](/wiki/Placental_mammals "Placental mammals") ([eutherians](/wiki/Eutherians "Eutherians")).[[23]](#cite_note-KentCarr-23)
Within placental mammals, the size and complexity of the neocortex increased over time. The area of the neocortex of mice is only about 1/100 that of monkeys, and that of monkeys is only about 1/10 that of humans.[[21]](#cite_note-MarkFBear-21) In addition, rats lack convolutions in their neocortex (possibly also because rats are small mammals), whereas cats have a moderate degree of convolutions, and humans have quite extensive convolutions.[[21]](#cite_note-MarkFBear-21) Extreme convolution of the neocortex is found in [dolphins](/wiki/Dolphin "Dolphin"), possibly related to their complex [echolocation](/wiki/Animal_echolocation "Animal echolocation").

## Clinical significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Clinical significance")]

### Diseases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_nervous_system&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Diseases")]

Main article: [Central nervous system disease](/wiki/Central_nervous_system_disease "Central nervous system disease")

There are many CNS diseases and conditions, including [infections](/wiki/List_of_central_nervous_system_infections "List of central nervous system infections") such as [encephalitis](/wiki/Encephalitis "Encephalitis") and [poliomyelitis](/wiki/Poliomyelitis "Poliomyelitis"), early-onset [neurological disorders](/wiki/Neurological_disorders "Neurological disorders") including [ADHD](/wiki/ADHD "ADHD") and [autism](/wiki/Autism "Autism"), seizure disorders such as [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy"), headache disorders such as [migraine](/wiki/Migraine "Migraine"), late-onset [neurodegenerative diseases](/wiki/Neurodegeneration "Neurodegeneration") such as [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease"), [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease"), and [essential tremor](/wiki/Essential_tremor "Essential tremor"), [autoimmune](/wiki/Autoimmune_disease "Autoimmune disease") and inflammatory diseases such as [multiple sclerosis](/wiki/Multiple_sclerosis "Multiple sclerosis") and [acute disseminated encephalomyelitis](/wiki/Acute_disseminated_encephalomyelitis "Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis"), genetic disorders such as [Krabbe's disease](/wiki/Krabbe%27s_disease "Krabbe's disease") and [Huntington's disease](/wiki/Huntington%27s_disease "Huntington's disease"), as well as [amyotrophic lateral sclerosis](/wiki/Amyotrophic_lateral_sclerosis "Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis") and [adrenoleukodystrophy](/wiki/Adrenoleukodystrophy "Adrenoleukodystrophy"). Lastly, cancers of the central nervous system can cause severe illness and, when [malignant](/wiki/Malignant_brain_tumor "Malignant brain tumor"), can have very high mortality rates. Symptoms depend on the size, growth rate, location and malignancy of tumors and can include alterations in motor control, hearing loss, headaches and changes in cognitive ability and autonomic functioning.

[Specialty](/wiki/Specialty_(medicine) "Specialty (medicine)") professional organizations recommend that neurological imaging of the brain be done only to answer a specific clinical question and not as routine screening.[[24]](#cite_note-ACRASNRcomtom-24)
